title: N-Queens Puzzle with Rotated Board and Overcounting

statement.markdown:
Given a size n, place n queens on a board of size n x n in such a way that no two queens attack each other. The board is rotated by 90 degrees counterclockwise compared to a standard chessboard.


You will be given a 2D array representing the board where a 1 at position [row][col] indicates a queen. You need to place the queens in such a way that they do not attack each other.


This problem is a bit different from the standard N-Queens problem as the board is rotated.


You can place a queen at the end of the board and it will not attack the queens in the current row because the board is rotated.


You can also place a queen at the current row and the current column and it will not attack the queens in the previous row because the board is rotated.


However, you cannot place a queen on a square that is diagonally adjacent to an already placed queen because the board is rotated.
statement.inputFormat: Input is of the size 2*n and contains two parts: the first part contains information about the existing queens on the board, and the second part contains the n for which we have to place the queens.
statement.outputFormat: For each test case, output the first line with n, and then output n numbers in each of the next n lines, indicating the positions of the queens.

examples.explanation: This is the only valid configuration

editorial.title: N-Queens Puzzle with Rotated Board and Overcounting
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved using backtracking. We need to place the queens in such a way that they do not attack each other. The board is rotated, so we need to consider the rotated version of the board.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to check every possible configuration and see if it is valid.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses backtracking to find the first valid configuration. We start by placing a queen at the end of the board and then try to place the remaining queens in such a way that they do not attack each other.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the fact that the backtracking algorithm tries every possible configuration and returns the first valid one.
editorial.dryRun: To perform a dry run, start by placing a queen at the end of the board. Then try to place the remaining queens in such a way that they do not attack each other.
commonMistakes: Not considering the rotated version of the board
Not using backtracking to find the first valid configuration

functionSignature.python: def solve(self, board: List[List[int]], n: int) -> List[List[int]]:
